projects.name: Surge
projects.description: Surge is a high-performance, open-source download manager built for power users, combining multi-connection and multi-mirror acceleration, smart chunking, failover handling, resumable downloads, and live progress tracking for much faster and more reliable file transfers. It also offers a polished terminal UI, headless server mode, CLI automation, and browser extension support, making it easy to manage downloads interactively or integrate them into automated workflows.

form.question: How to support
form.response: Star the repo, open issues, submit PRs, or spread the word. If you want to go further, you can sponsor us via GitHub Sponsors — every bit helps keep the project alive and growing.

form.question: A small brief about your project
form.response: Surge is a concurrent download manager written in Go that achieves 1.38x higher throughput than aria2c by chunk-splitting across up to 32 parallel connections. It runs as a background daemon with a token-protected HTTP API, a real-time Bubble Tea TUI, and a browser extension — all funneling into a single centralized engine.

form.question: One FOSS maintainer lesson for your younger self
form.response: Ship something broken early rather than something perfect never. The feedback you get from real users in week one is worth more than six months of solo over-engineering. Also Reddit is a goldmine for getting your project out there

form.question: Why do you do it? Why do you bother maintaining a FOSS project?
form.response: I wanted a download manager that was actually fast and didn't get in my way — so I built one. Turns out building it taught me more about Go, concurrency, and systems programming than anything else I've done. That loop of building → breaking → fixing is so addictive. Plus, there is a community of like minded people out there who are building cool stuff, and you can be a part of it too.

form.question: If your repo had a theme song, what would it be?
Which file in your project would you most like to set on fire?
form.response: internal/tui/ - This was never meant to be used by real users, and it shows.

form.question: What's your open-source villain origin story?
form.response: I was 9 when my old laptop just couldn't play games anymore on Windows. Nuked my drive a couple of times, installed Ubuntu 16.04, and never looked back. That's when I realised software could be whatever I want it to be. And that's exactly what I've been doing ever since.

form.question: If you had to use one emoji to convey what it is like to be a FOSS maintainer, what would it be?
